original_abstract: To access architecturally complex natural products, chemists usually devise a customized synthetic strategy for constructing a single target skeleton. In contrast, biosynthetic assembly lines often employ divergent intramolecular cyclizations of a polyunsaturated common intermediate to produce diverse arrays of scaffolds. With the aim of integrating such biogenetic strategies, here we show the development of an artificial divergent assembly line generating unprecedented numbers of scaffold variations of terpenoid indole alkaloids. This approach allows not only practical access to multipotent intermediates but also systematic diversification of skeletal, stereochemical, and functional group properties without structural simplification of naturally occurring alkaloids. Three distinct modes of [4+2] cyclizations and two types of redox-mediated annulations provided divergent access to five skeletally distinct scaffolds involving iboga-, aspidosperma-, andranginine-, and ngouniensine-type skeletons and a non-natural variant within 6–9 steps from tryptamine. The efficiency of our approach was demonstrated by successful total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine, (±)-andranginine, and (−)-catharanthine. Natural products often bear a variety of functional groups on a rigid, architecturally complex, and sp3-rich skeleton, which allows specific molecular recognition through multi-point interactions to modulate the functions of target biomacromolecules. Chemical synthesis of natural products and their analogs could provide optimum screening collections for the development of drug candidates
humanized_abstract: Unlike chemists who need to devise a special synthesis to gain access to an architecturally complex natural product, in Nature, chemists (biosynthetic enzymes) can start with a relatively simple molecule (such as a common precursor polyunsaturated intermediate) and use a variety of divergent intramolecular processes (such as multiple types of intramolecular Diels-Alder reactions and intramolecular reductive couplings) to give many different structural scaffolds. In an effort to marry the efficiency of natural biosynthetic pathways to the power of chemical synthesis, we demonstrated the preparation of an artificial divergent heterocyclic polyketomacrocycle assembly line that enables rapid access to an unprecedented number of analogs of terpenoid indole alkaloids with diverse skeletal configurations, extensive variations of stereocenters, and varying number and types of functional groups. Access to multipotent intermediates as well as a flexible route to highly divergent analogs are achieved without requiring any structural simplification to known natural products. Our [4+2] cycloadditions which were achieved through three distinct mechanisms: as formal hetero-Diels-Alder, hetero-[4+2] annulation between two unsaturated heteroaromatics, or hetero-homo-[4+2] upon reacting an unsaturated heteroaromatic with a heteroatomic olefin; as well as the two redox-mediated intramolecular hetero-annulations at unsaturated heteroaromatic precursors afforded five distinct skeletal configurations corresponding to iboga-, aspidosperma-, andranginine-, and ngouniensine-type skeletons as well as one novel unnatural skeletal configuration. Syntheses with yields ranging from 45 to 85% took 6-9 steps from tryptamine depending on the target product. Syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine, (±)-andranginine, and (−)-catharanthine are illustrated. Natural products generally display the following characteristics that impart to them their bioactivities: a wide variety of functional groups, on the relatively rigid and complex (aromatic and sp3) three-dimensional structural framework. These structural characteristics enables a natural product to recognize with high affinity a large number of bio-macromolecules by making contacts with several of their critical structural features. This allows for the optimization of drug candidate lead structures in short order through the efficient access of large numbers of synthetic analogs through proven multiple routes.
